title: Algebra
blurb: Equations, inequalities, systems, exponents ‚Äî the moves behind a third of any exam.

forms.title: Two-step linear equations
forms.skill: Undo addition, then undo multiplication ‚Äî in that order.

variants.explanation: Move the constant first: $3x = 15$, then divide by $3$: $x = 5$. Check: $3 \cdot 5 + 5 = 20$ ‚úì.
Move the constant first: $4x = 20$, then divide by $4$: $x = 5$. Check: $4 \cdot 5 - 7 = 13$ ‚úì.
Move the constant first: $5x = 35$, then divide by $5$: $x = 7$. Check: $5 \cdot 7 + 8 = 43$ ‚úì.
Move the constant first: $7x = 28$, then divide by $7$: $x = 4$. Check: $7 \cdot 4 - 4 = 24$ ‚úì.
Multiplying powers adds exponents ($5+3=8$); dividing subtracts ($8-4=4$). One add, one subtract ‚Äî never multiply the exponents.
Multiplying powers adds exponents ($7+2=9$); dividing subtracts ($9-6=3$). One add, one subtract ‚Äî never multiply the exponents.
Multiplying powers adds exponents ($4+5=9$); dividing subtracts ($9-3=6$). One add, one subtract ‚Äî never multiply the exponents.
Multiplying powers adds exponents ($6+4=10$); dividing subtracts ($10-8=2$). One add, one subtract ‚Äî never multiply the exponents.
Square first, multiply second: $2 \cdot 3^2 = 18$, then subtract $3 \cdot 4 = 12$: answer $6$. The classic slip is $(2a)^2$ ‚Äî the square belongs to $a$ alone.
Square first, multiply second: $3 \cdot 2^2 = 12$, then subtract $2 \cdot 5 = 10$: answer $2$. The classic slip is $(3a)^2$ ‚Äî the square belongs to $a$ alone.
Square first, multiply second: $2 \cdot 4^2 = 32$, then subtract $5 \cdot 3 = 15$: answer $17$. The classic slip is $(2a)^2$ ‚Äî the square belongs to $a$ alone.
Square first, multiply second: $5 \cdot 3^2 = 45$, then subtract $2 \cdot 2 = 4$: answer $41$. The classic slip is $(5a)^2$ ‚Äî the square belongs to $a$ alone.

forms.title: Inequalities with a sign flip
forms.skill: Dividing by a negative reverses the inequality.

variants.explanation: Subtract $20$: $-4x < -12$. Dividing by $-4$ FLIPS the inequality: $x > 3$. Test $x = 4$: $20 - 16 < 8$ ‚úì.
Subtract $15$: $-3x < -9$. Dividing by $-3$ FLIPS the inequality: $x > 3$. Test $x = 4$: $15 - 12 < 6$ ‚úì.
Subtract $30$: $-5x < -20$. Dividing by $-5$ FLIPS the inequality: $x > 4$. Test $x = 5$: $30 - 25 < 10$ ‚úì.
Subtract $14$: $-2x < -8$. Dividing by $-2$ FLIPS the inequality: $x > 4$. Test $x = 5$: $14 - 10 < 6$ ‚úì.

forms.title: Systems of two equations
forms.skill: Eliminate one variable; always check the pair in BOTH equations.

variants.statement: The system $\begin{cases} x + y = 5 \\ 2x - y = 4 \end{cases}$ has solution $(x; y)$. Find $x + y$.
variants.explanation: Adding or substituting gives $x = 3$, $y = 2$ (check both equations!), so $x + y = 5$.

variants.statement: The system $\begin{cases} x + y = 5 \\ x - y = 3 \end{cases}$ has solution $(x; y)$. Find $x + y$.
variants.explanation: Adding or substituting gives $x = 4$, $y = 1$ (check both equations!), so $x + y = 5$.

variants.statement: The system $\begin{cases} 2x + y = 9 \\ x - y = -3 \end{cases}$ has solution $(x; y)$. Find $x + y$.
variants.explanation: Adding or substituting gives $x = 2$, $y = 5$ (check both equations!), so $x + y = 7$.

variants.statement: The system $\begin{cases} x + 2y = 11 \\ x - y = 2 \end{cases}$ has solution $(x; y)$. Find $x + y$.
variants.explanation: Adding or substituting gives $x = 5$, $y = 3$ (check both equations!), so $x + y = 8$.

forms.title: Rational equations
forms.skill: Clear the denominator, solve, then verify the denominator isn't zero.

variants.explanation: Multiply both sides by $x + 2$ (allowed since $x \ne -2$): $x + 6 = 3x + 6$, so $2x = 0$ and $x = 0$. Check the denominator: $0 + 2 \ne 0$ ‚úì.
Multiply both sides by $x + 2$ (allowed since $x \ne -2$): $x + 8 = 4x + 8$, so $3x = 0$ and $x = 0$. Check the denominator: $0 + 2 \ne 0$ ‚úì.
Multiply both sides by $x + 4$ (allowed since $x \ne -4$): $x + 10 = 3x + 12$, so $2x = -2$ and $x = -1$. Check the denominator: $-1 + 4 \ne 0$ ‚úì.
Multiply both sides by $x + 3$ (allowed since $x \ne -3$): $x + 9 = 4x + 12$, so $3x = -3$ and $x = -1$. Check the denominator: $-1 + 3 \ne 0$ ‚úì.

forms.title: Absolute-value inequalities
forms.skill: |x ‚àí a| ‚â§ b is a distance statement: a ¬± b bracket the answer.

variants.explanation: $|x - 3| \le 5$ means the distance from $x$ to $3$ is at most $5$: $3 - 5 \le x \le 3 + 5$, i.e. $[-2; 8]$. The 'or' split belongs to $\ge$, not $\le$.
$|x - 2| \le 7$ means the distance from $x$ to $2$ is at most $7$: $2 - 7 \le x \le 2 + 7$, i.e. $[-5; 9]$. The 'or' split belongs to $\ge$, not $\le$.
$|x - 4| \le 3$ means the distance from $x$ to $4$ is at most $3$: $4 - 3 \le x \le 4 + 3$, i.e. $[1; 7]$. The 'or' split belongs to $\ge$, not $\le$.
$|x - 1| \le 6$ means the distance from $x$ to $1$ is at most $6$: $1 - 6 \le x \le 1 + 6$, i.e. $[-5; 7]$. The 'or' split belongs to $\ge$, not $\le$.

forms.title: Systems with a parameter
forms.skill: No solution ‚ü∫ slopes match, intercepts don't (proportional coefficients, unproportional constants).

variants.statement: For which value of $m$ does the system $\begin{cases} x + 2y = 3 \\ 2x + my = 5 \end{cases}$ have NO solution?
variants.explanation: No solution = parallel lines: coefficients proportional but constants not. $\frac{2}{1} = \frac{m}{2}$ gives $m = 4$ ‚Äî and $\frac{5}{3} \ne \frac{2}{1}$ confirms they never meet.

variants.statement: For which value of $m$ does the system $\begin{cases} x + 3y = 4 \\ 3x + my = 7 \end{cases}$ have NO solution?
variants.explanation: No solution = parallel lines: coefficients proportional but constants not. $\frac{3}{1} = \frac{m}{3}$ gives $m = 9$ ‚Äî and $\frac{7}{4} \ne \frac{3}{1}$ confirms they never meet.

variants.statement: For which value of $m$ does the system $\begin{cases} 2x + 1y = 5 \\ 4x + my = 8 \end{cases}$ have NO solution?
variants.explanation: No solution = parallel lines: coefficients proportional but constants not. $\frac{4}{2} = \frac{m}{1}$ gives $m = 2$ ‚Äî and $\frac{8}{5} \ne \frac{4}{2}$ confirms they never meet.

variants.statement: For which value of $m$ does the system $\begin{cases} x + 4y = 6 \\ 2x + my = 9 \end{cases}$ have NO solution?
variants.explanation: No solution = parallel lines: coefficients proportional but constants not. $\frac{2}{1} = \frac{m}{4}$ gives $m = 8$ ‚Äî and $\frac{9}{6} \ne \frac{2}{1}$ confirms they never meet.
